items.q: Do electrons travel from the battery to the bulb at nearly the speed of light?
items.correct: No — electron drift velocity is tiny (around mm/s); it is the electric field that is established quickly.
items.misconception: Yes — electrons race around the circuit at light speed.
items.random_wrong: No — electrons stay still and protons move instead.
items.why: A bulb lights almost instantly, so students assume electrons move that fast; the signal is fast, the charges drift slowly.

items.q: Is current used up as it passes through a bulb?
items.correct: No — the current is the same on both sides of the bulb; energy, not charge, is transferred.
items.misconception: Yes — the bulb consumes some of the current.
items.random_wrong: Yes — the current evaporates as light.
items.why: Students conflate current with energy; charge is conserved all the way around a series circuit.

items.q: Does doubling the light intensity increase each photoelectron's maximum kinetic energy?
items.correct: No — it increases the number emitted per second; KEmax depends only on frequency and the work function.
items.misconception: Yes — more intense light produces faster electrons.
items.random_wrong: Yes — intensity raises the work function.
items.why: The beam-energy versus photon-energy confusion is the classic photoelectric misconception Einstein's model resolved.

items.correct: The rate of flow of electric charge
items.misconception: The number of electrons in a wire
items.why: Correct answer: The rate of flow of electric charge. “The number of electrons in a wire” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The energy gained by an electron accelerated through a p.d. of 1 volt
items.why: It's The energy gained by an electron accelerated through a p.d. of 1 volt. “The charge of one electron” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In Einstein's photoelectric equation hf = φ + KEmax, the symbol φ represents:
items.correct: The work function of the metal
items.misconception: The kinetic energy of the photon
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The work function of the metal. “The kinetic energy of the photon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A current of 2 A flows for 10 s. The charge transferred is:
items.why: It's 20 C. “0.2 C” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A current of 3 A flows for 60 s. How much charge is transferred?
items.why: The real figure is 180 C. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
